medications.indication_rationale: A mild topical corticosteroid to the localised itchy/burning target lesions is appropriate symptomatic care for erythema multiforme minor — the source note's prescribed treatment. Hydrocortisone 1% is available over the counter in Australia, so over telehealth the AI Doctor can recognise and recommend it as symptomatic care while arranging review.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply a thin layer to the affected/itchy target lesions twice daily as needed (per source note); avoid applying to any broken or blistered centres
contraindications_in_this_case: Avoid applying to broken, weeping or blistered lesion centres
Stop and seek review if the skin worsens, blisters spread, or signs of secondary infection develop

medications.indication_rationale: Simple analgesia as needed for the burning discomfort is a reasonable, low-risk adjunct per the source note ('advised simple analgesia if required'). It does not treat the underlying eruption.
medications.dose_route_frequency: standard over-the-counter adult dosing as needed for discomfort (no dose specified in the source note; do not synthesise a regimen)

medications.indication_rationale: A single, mild, self-limiting erythema multiforme minor does not warrant systemic corticosteroids — their benefit is unproven for mild EM and they carry unnecessary risk. Reaching for oral steroids here is over-treatment of a benign eruption. (Systemic corticosteroids belong to the assessment of severe/major disease under specialist care, not this presentation.)

medications.drug_name: Oral antiviral (e.g. aciclovir/valaciclovir) for the acute episode
medications.indication_rationale: Antiviral treatment does not alter the course of an established acute erythema multiforme episode and the triggering cold sore is already healing, so it is not indicated acutely. Antiviral SUPPRESSION (prophylaxis) is a consideration only for frequently RECURRENT HSV-associated erythema multiforme, to be decided at review — not started in this single acute encounter.

medications.drug_name: Topical antihistamine or topical local-anaesthetic 'itch' cream
medications.indication_rationale: Topical antihistamines and local-anaesthetic creams are themselves frequent contact sensitisers and can complicate an inflammatory dermatosis — an error of commission. Use a mild topical corticosteroid and simple oral analgesia (± a sedating oral antihistamine at night if needed) instead.

Erythema multiforme is an immune-mediated, non-infective eruption of the skin — there is no bacterial infection to treat. Antibiotics are not indicated and would be over-treatment; reserve strictly for a documented secondary bacterial infection if one later develops.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply cool, damp washcloths to the hot, burning target lesions instead of scratching them — this calms the nerve endings and protects the skin surface. Do NOT pop or pick the small blisters in the centre of the targets.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Breaks the itch/burn-scratch cycle, preserves the skin barrier and reduces the risk of accidental damage or secondary infection — the source note's specified positive behavioural change.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Understand and hold onto the fact that this is the body's dramatic-but-safe immune response to the recent cold sore; expect the rash to peak and then fade over 1-3 weeks, and let it settle rather than seeking repeated urgent assessments.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reassurance is a core part of management for this highly anxious patient — understanding the benign, self-limiting nature reduces distress and avoidable emergency presentations, as emphasised in the source note.

patient_education_points.point: These 'bullseye' spots are erythema multiforme — an allergic-type immune reaction to the cold-sore (herpes) virus you had about 10 days ago. Your immune system is reacting to fragments of the virus; it is NOT a live infection in the skin and it is not dangerous.
It is a common, self-limiting reaction. The rash has likely peaked; it will gradually darken and fade over the next 1-3 weeks and may leave temporary dark marks that eventually resolve.

For comfort: use cool damp compresses instead of scratching, apply the mild steroid cream thinly to itchy spots, and take simple pain relief if needed. Do not pop the small blisters in the centre of the spots.

Seek urgent help if things change: swelling of your face, lips or throat or any difficulty breathing (call 000); painful sores or ulcers in your mouth, eyes or genitals, or the skin starting to peel off in sheets with a high fever (go to an emergency department the same day); or new eye pain, redness or vision problems (urgent same-day review).

follow_up_plan.interval: routine GP review within 7-10 days to confirm resolution and lay eyes on the skin and mucosae, or sooner if the patient wants earlier reassurance or if symptoms change
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: resolution of the target lesions, confirmation of the erythema multiforme minor diagnosis, absence of any mucosal involvement, and whether HSV suppression is warranted if episodes recur
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: mucosal sores in the mouth, eyes or genitals; skin peeling in sheets or high fever; new eye symptoms; or facial/airway swelling or breathing difficulty — seek same-day/emergency care

must_include_items: Recognise the target/iris lesions plus the preceding cold sore (HSV) as erythema multiforme minor — a common, benign, self-limiting reaction, not an emergency

Actively screen for and safety-net the severe-spectrum mimic (SJS/TEN — mucosal erosions, skin peeling, high fever) and anaphylaxis (facial/airway swelling, breathing difficulty), with explicit T4-T5 triggers
Provide strong reassurance about the benign, self-limiting nature and the 1-3 week course
Advise patient-performable symptomatic care: cool damp compresses instead of scratching, do NOT pop the central blisters, a mild topical corticosteroid and simple analgesia for comfort
Arrange review (routine GP review within ~7-10 days, or sooner if desired) to confirm the minor variant and inspect the mucosae
acceptable_alternatives: A sedating oral antihistamine at night to aid sleep/comfort is an acceptable adjunct

Prompt/same-day (T2-T3) review to lay eyes on the mucosae and confirm it is not SJS/TEN is an acceptable, slightly more cautious disposition given the rapid spread and patient distress, and is not scored as an error
Deferring any steroid/antiviral decision to the reviewing clinician rather than specifying a regimen is acceptable
Raising HSV suppressive antiviral therapy as a consideration for the future IF episodes recur (decided at review) is acceptable
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen for/safety-net SJS/TEN (mucosal involvement, skin peeling, high fever) before committing to outpatient management
Failing to safety-net evolving anaphylaxis / airway angioedema
Failing to provide reassurance to a highly anxious patient
Failing to arrange any review in an acute spreading eruption (pure self-care only with no safety-net)
errors_of_commission: Reflexively escalating a systemically-well patient with a negative red-flag screen straight to 000/ED (over-triage)
Prescribing systemic corticosteroids or acute antivirals for a single benign erythema multiforme minor
Recommending a topical antihistamine or topical local-anaesthetic 'itch' cream (contact sensitisers)
Prescribing empiric oral antibiotics for a non-infective eruption
Advising the patient to pop/deroof the central blisters
Dismissing the presentation as trivial and giving no reassurance, symptomatic advice, follow-up or safety-net
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (a) recognising erythema multiforme minor from the target lesions + HSV trigger and reassuring the patient, (b) an explicit safety-net for SJS/TEN (T4) and anaphylaxis/airway (T5), and (c) advising symptomatic care (cool compresses + do-not-pop + mild topical steroid ± analgesia) and arranging review. Reflexive escalation to 000/ED, systemic steroids/antibiotics for a benign minor episode, a topical-antihistamine answer, or advising blister-popping is a scored error regardless of other content; so is giving no reassurance, treatment, follow-up or safety-net at all.
